name: System design foundations
description: CAP theorem and classic design exercises (URL shortener, media streaming). From the 'system design' prep doc.

topics.name: CAP theorem
topics.description: Consistency, availability, partition tolerance — pick two under a network partition.
topics.studyNotes:
The three letters: Consistency — every read sees the latest write; Availability — every request gets a response; Partition tolerance — the system keeps working when the network drops messages between nodes.

The theorem's real content: partitions are not optional — networks fail — so P is mandatory, and the actual choice, DURING a partition, is C versus A. Refuse requests to stay consistent (CP), or keep answering with possibly stale data (AP).

Ground it in systems: a bank ledger is CP — better to refuse a withdrawal than allow a double-spend; a shopping cart is AP — better to accept the item and reconcile later than block the customer.

The refinement that scores senior points — PACELC: even WITHOUT a partition, there is a trade-off between latency and consistency (Else: L vs C). Synchronous replication buys consistency with latency; asynchronous buys speed with staleness. So the full statement: under Partition, A vs C; Else, L vs C. Per-operation nuance beats a single system-wide label: the same product can be CP for payments and AP for its dashboard.

topics.name: URL shortener design
topics.description: Designing a bit.ly-style service: key generation, storage, redirects, scale.
topics.studyNotes:
The bit.ly design — a compact tour of read-heavy system design.

Start by clarifying scale: how many new links/day, and the read/write ratio — redirects outnumber creations by orders of magnitude, so the system is optimized for reads. Ask about custom aliases and expiry; they change the key story.

Key generation options: base62-encode an auto-increment ID (short, no collisions, but guessable and needs a central counter), hash the URL and truncate (collision handling required), or a key-generation service handing out pre-generated batches (scales cleanly). Know one well and name the alternatives.

Storage: the access pattern is a pure key → value lookup, so a key-value/NoSQL store fits and scales horizontally; relational adds nothing the lookup needs.

The read path is where the design wins: cache hot short-codes (Redis, and a CDN in front) so a redirect never touches the database for popular links. The redirect itself is a 301/302 — permanent caches harder, temporary keeps analytics.

Analytics without slowing redirects: never write the click synchronously — emit an event to a queue and let a consumer aggregate; the redirect stays a cache hit plus a fire-and-forget publish.

questions.title: CAP theorem
questions.prompt: Explain the CAP theorem. What does it actually force you to choose in a distributed system?
questions.answerStructureHint: Define C/A/P, the partition is a given, so the real choice is consistency vs availability during a partition.

expectedPoints.label: Defines consistency, availability, partition tolerance
expectedPoints.description: C = every read sees the latest write, A = every request gets a response, P = system keeps working despite dropped messages.

expectedPoints.label: Partition is unavoidable, so it's C vs A
expectedPoints.description: Since partitions happen, the real decision under a partition is consistency vs availability.

expectedPoints.label: Grounds it in real systems
expectedPoints.description: Gives a CP vs AP example (e.g. a bank ledger vs a shopping cart / eventually consistent store).

followUps.prompt: Even without a partition there is a trade-off. What is it (the 'else' in PACELC)?

questions.title: Design a URL shortener
questions.prompt: Design a URL shortening service like bit.ly. Walk me through the key decisions.
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify scale and read/write ratio, key generation, storage, fast redirects with caching, then analytics/expiry.

expectedPoints.label: Clarifies scale and read/write ratio
expectedPoints.description: Asks about traffic, that reads vastly outnumber writes, custom aliases, expiry.

expectedPoints.label: Key generation strategy
expectedPoints.description: Base62 of an ID / counter, hashing with collision handling, or a key-generation service.

expectedPoints.label: Caching for fast redirects
expectedPoints.description: Cache hot short-codes (and use a CDN) so the redirect lookup is fast under read-heavy load.

expectedPoints.label: Appropriate storage choice
expectedPoints.description: A simple key-value / NoSQL store fits the lookup pattern and scales horizontally.

followUps.prompt: The product wants click analytics per link without slowing down redirects. How would you do it?
